datasets.name: report_divergence
queryLines:
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN kpi LIKE 'Total Charges%' THEN CONCAT('Total Charges (', CHR(36), ' in millions)')
    WHEN kpi LIKE 'Net Revenue%' THEN CONCAT('Net Revenue (', CHR(36), ' in millions)')
    ELSE kpi
  END as kpi,
  report_a_cfo,
  report_b_revops,
  divergence,
  root_cause,
  explanation,
  CASE kpi
    WHEN 'Net Collection Rate (%)' THEN 95.0
    WHEN 'Denial Rate (%)' THEN 5.0
    WHEN 'Days in AR' THEN 40.0
    WHEN 'Commercial Payer Mix (%)' THEN 45.0
    WHEN 'Total Charges ()' THEN 57.0
    WHEN 'Net Revenue ()' THEN 24.0
  END as target
FROM
  serverless_stable_jc9zgx_catalog.radiology_revenue_cycle.v_report_divergence

pages.name: 58fcbef9
pages.displayName: Executive Summary — Two Reports, One Truth

lines:
## Two Reports, One Truth

Same data, different business logic, divergent KPIs. 

**Report A** (Finance/CFO): Conservative, exclusion-based — "Only count what we can validate"

**Report B** (Revenue Ops): Inclusive, count-everything — "Count everything until proven invalid"

frame.title: Report A vs Report B — KPI Comparison

lines:
## KPI Definitions

**Net Collection Rate** — Of money payers agreed to pay, what % was actually collected. Measures billing efficiency. **Target: ≥95%**

**Denial Rate** — % of claims rejected by payers on first pass. Each denial requires rework or write-off. **Target: <5%**

**Days in AR** — Average days a charge sits unpaid from service to payment. Measures cash velocity. **Target: <40 days**

**Commercial Payer Mix** — Share from commercial insurers vs. Medicare/Medicaid. Commercial pays 2–4x government rates. **Target: ≥45%**

**Total Charges** — Gross billed amount before adjustments. Reflects volume and fee schedule pricing. **Target: ≥$57M** (annual budget)

**Net Revenue** — Expected receipts after contractual adjustments. Closest proxy to real cash flow. **Target: ≥$24M** (annual budget)

## Root Cause — 8 Data Quality Defects Across 6 Dimensions

The **same underlying tables** are queried by both reports — the difference is how each team handles ambiguous or defective data.

### COMPLETENESS **Calculation**: `Collection Rate = Payments Received ÷ Allowed Amount` **Defect**: 8% of *paid* claims have NULL `allowed_amount` — concentrated on more exepensive procedures like MRI/NM/IR **Business context**: If a claim is paid, the payer determined an allowed amount. NULL means the ERA/835 remittance wasn't matched to the claim — common on complex modalities where manual payments, appeals, or carve-out networks bypass standard electronic posting. **The real question**: Why don't 8% of paid claims have remittance data? Both teams work around a revenue posting pipeline failure instead of fixing it. **Report A**: Excludes these rows from the denominator
**Report B**: Substitutes the billed charge when `allowed_amount` is NULL. Since charges are 3–5x what payers actually allow, this massively inflates the denominator and deflates the collection rate. **Result**: Net Collection Rate diverges **6.5pp**

### VALIDITY **Calculation**: `Denial Rate = Denied Claims ÷ Total Claims Submitted` **Defect**: 30% of denied claims have invalid CARC codes (CO-999, PI-300, OA-500) that don't exist in the X12 835 standard **Business context**: Invalid CARC codes typically arise from clearinghouse translation errors or payer system migrations. When a payer upgrades their adjudication platform, denial reason codes temporarily map to placeholder values. Smaller payers and regional clearinghouses are especially prone during system transitions. **The real question**: Why are invalid codes persisting without correction? There's no validation on ingest and no feedback loop with the clearinghouse to fix broken mappings. **Report A**: Drops invalid CARCs — claims with ONLY invalid denials disappear from denied count
**Report B**: Counts ALL denials regardless of code validity **Result**: Denial Rate diverges **3.9pp**

### TIMELINESS **Calculation**: `Days in AR = Avg Days from Date of Service to Payment` **Defect**: Hospital-based locations (system\_a) have AR snapshots 9 days stale (last: May 6 vs today: May 15) **Business context**: Hospital-based radiology relies on the hospital's HIS/RIS to push AR data via scheduled batch feeds. When hospital IT deprioritizes the feed (maintenance windows, system upgrades, staff turnover), data goes stale silently. Freestanding centers control their own systems so feeds stay current. **The real question**: Why is there no SLA monitoring or alerting on feed freshness? Both teams make decisions without knowing they're missing 9 days of hospital data. **Report A**: Uses MAX(snapshot\_date) per location — includes hospitals' low-AR data (avg 22 days)
**Report B**: Filters to snapshot\_date = TODAY — excludes hospitals entirely, leaving only freestanding (avg 60 days) **Result**: Days in AR diverges **13 days**

### CONSISTENCY **Calculation**: `Commercial Mix = Commercial Claims ÷ Total Claims` **Defect**: `payer_name` has 3–5 variants per payer (e.g., "BCBS", "Blue Cross BS", "BCBS IL", "BlueCross BlueShield of IL") **Business context**: Payer names enter at multiple touchpoints — registration, eligibility verification, ERA posting — each using different conventions. Mergers make it worse (CVS acquired Aetna but systems still reference both). Without master data management for payer identity, variants accumulate unchecked. **The real question**: The canonical payer table exists (Report A uses it). Why isn't it enforced at registration so variants never enter the system? **Report A**: JOINs to canonical `payer` table on `payer_id` — correct classification
**Report B**: Pattern-matches on `payer_name` with incomplete lookup — misses "UHC", "CVS/Aetna", etc. **Result**: Commercial Mix diverges **19.5pp**

### UNIQUENESS + ACCURACY **Calculation**: `Total Charges = Sum of All Billed Amounts` **Defect 1**: 3% of pre-Oct 2025 claims are unflagged rebill duplicates
**Defect 2**: 1.5% have TC/26 component overlap (global + technical + professional for same CPT) **Business context**: Rebills (corrected claims) are normal — modifier changes, late charge capture, auth corrections. Before Oct 2025, the billing system didn't flag them, so originals and corrections coexist without linkage. TC/26 overlap occurs during transitions between billing arrangements (e.g., radiologist moves from employed to independent). **The real question**: Why did rebill tracking fail for 5+ months before being fixed? And why does charge capture allow both global and component billing on the same encounter? **Report A**: Deduplicates by (encounter, patient, charge); removes components when global exists
**Report B**: Sums all claim lines naively **Result**: Total Charges diverges **$4.5M**

### ACCURACY **Calculation**: `Net Revenue = Sum of Allowed Amounts` **Defect B**: PAY-011/012 clearinghouse error sets `allowed = charge` (should be 40–60%)
**Defect C**: BCBS Jan–Feb 2026 uses stale fee schedule — `allowed` is 12% below actual payment **Business context**: Clearinghouse errors happen when the EDI translation layer fails to parse contractual adjustments from the 835 — the system defaults to charge as a fallback. Stale fee schedules occur when contract renegotiations complete (new rates effective Jan 1) but the contract management system isn't updated. **The real question**: Why is there no reconciliation between allowed amounts and actual payments? A simple variance alert (payment ≠ expected allowed) would catch both issues immediately. **Report A**: Reconciles against `payment` table; uses actual payments for affected payers
**Report B**: Trusts `allowed_amount` at face value **Result**: Net Revenue diverges **$4.1M**

frame.title: Divergence Magnitude by DQ Dimension
frame.description: Higher bars = greater impact from the data quality defect

lines:
## Business Impact — Cost of Inaction

When the CEO receives diverging reports, the organization enters a 6–8 week reconciliation cycle involving 12 people across 6 phases. Below is the realistic cost of resolving data quality issues *reactively* instead of preventing them.

frame.title: Resource Allocation by Role → Phase
frame.description: Width of each flow represents hours spent. Investigation dominates.

frame.title: Cumulative Cost by Phase
frame.description: Watch costs accumulate. Investigation is the largest single phase.

lines:
## The Alternative: Proactive Data Quality

| | Reactive (Current) | Proactive (With DQ Monitoring) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Time to detect | 6–8 weeks (CEO finds it) | < 1 hour (automated alert) |
| People involved | 12 | 1–2 (data engineer + owner) |
| Cost | $31K+ labor | < $500 (automated fix) |
| Decision delay | 1 quarter | None |
| Trust erosion | Finance vs. Ops conflict | Shared single source of truth |

**With a shared metrics layer and automated DQ checks**, the divergence never reaches the CEO — it's caught at ingest, flagged, and resolved before reports are generated.
